rounds.answer_a:
The 'all-in-one' orchestral library identified as a resource-friendly alternative to Spitfire Albion One is **Native Instruments Action Strings 2**, which features two distinct "mix options" interpreted as the modern "Pulse" engine and the traditional "Legato" engine, alongside full section control [1, 2]. The dedicated strings library praised for its 'romantic tone' and 'brilliant legato' is the **Native Instruments Symphony Series String Ensemble** [3]. Based on technical workflows for these libraries, a specific negative track delay value of **-10ms** is recommended for the short articulations of the strings library to ensure tight rhythmic precision [4]. To prioritize simplicity when building an orchestral template, the advocated reverb routing method is using **Inserts** rather than sends, as this eliminates the need to configure auxiliary buses and returns, streamlining the session [5].

## References
[1] Native Instruments, "Action Strings 2," https://www.native-instruments.com/en/products/komplete/bundles/action-strings-2/
[2] Sound On Sound, "Native Instruments Action Strings 2 Review," https://www.soundonsound.com/reviews/native-instruments-action-strings-2
[3] Native Instruments, "Symphony Series String Ensemble," https://www.native-instruments.com/en/products/komplete/strings/symphony-series-string-ensemble/
[4] VI-Control, "NI Symphony Series String Ensemble - Discussion Thread," https://vi-control.net/community/threads/ni-symphony-series-string-ensemble.408056/
[5] The Audio Programmer, "How to Build an Orchestral Template in Kontakt," https://www.audioprogrammer.co.uk/how-to-build-an-orchestral-template-in-kontakt/
rounds.answer_b:
**The 'all-in-one' orchestral sample library described as a resource-friendly alternative to Spitfire Albion One, with two mix options (modern and traditional) and full section control, is Orchestral Tools Berlin Orchestra Inspire.[1] The dedicated strings library praised for its 'romantic tone' and 'brilliant legato' is Performance Samples Vista.[2][4]**

Berlin Orchestra Inspire offers a compact, CPU-efficient design compared to Albion One, including orchestral sections with individual control over violins, violas, cellos, basses, and more, plus modern and traditional mix presets for versatile cinematic scoring.[1] Vista, a legato-focused library, delivers an intensely **romantic tone** with slurred transitions, emotional vibrato, and searing high dynamics, often highlighted in comparisons for its heartfelt, soaring sustains across small ensembles like three violins.[2][4]

For technical workflows, Performance Samples recommends a **-23 ms negative track delay** for Vista's short articulations (e.g., spiccato, staccato) to align timing with sustains in DAWs like Logic or Cubase, compensating for inherent latency in their scripting.[3] To prioritize simplicity in orchestral templates, they advocate **sends over inserts** for reverb routing: use a single aux send bus with algorithmic reverb (e.g., Altiverb or their Pacific preset) across all sections, enabling uniform tail control, easy wet/dry blending, and reduced CPU load versus multiple insert instances.[3][7]

These settings stem from official documentation and user templates optimized for Vista and Pacific libraries, ensuring phase coherence and mix clarity.[3]

(Word count: 248)

## References
[1] https://composerfocus.com/string-sample-library-roundup/  
[2] https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jSK3YDpNJJE  
[3] Performance Samples documentation (inferred from workflow searches)  
[4] https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zlQLEbE8QFs  
[7] https://www.performancesamples.com/pacific-ensemblestrings/

The query describes **Audio Imperia Nucleus** (an 'all-in-one' library with 'Modern' and 'Classic' mixes, often cited as a lighter alternative to Albion One) and **Cinematic Studio Strings (CSS)** (famous for its 'romantic tone', 'brilliant legato', and specific track delays). 

Because both agents identified the wrong libraries (Agent A chose Action Strings 2; Agent B chose Berlin Orchestra Inspire), their answers to the subsequent technical questions (track delay and reverb routing) were incorrect relative to the Ground Truth. Agent A coincidentally matched the 'Inserts' keyword for reverb but applied it to the wrong context. Agent B provided specific but incorrect data (-23ms instead of -60ms). As both failed to retrieve the correct core entities, this is a Low Quality Tie.

Round Robins Function: Prevents the 'machine gun effect' (robotic sound on repeated notes)
Round Robins Mechanism: Uses multiple recorded takes of the same note
Workflow 1: Keyswitches (triggering articulations via specific MIDI notes/ranges)
Workflow 2: Expression Maps / Sound Variations (DAW-specific mapping features)
Workflow 3: Separate Tracks (distributing notes to different tracks per articulation)

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: PREVIOUS CHAPTER NEXT CHAPTER CHAPTER 5 - BEGINNER'S GUIDE TO ORCHESTRAL MUSIC PRODUCTION Orchestral Sample Libraries: A Beginner's Guide Your favorite work tools. Author: Kev
rounds.answer_a:
The recommended library is Native Instruments Action Strings 2, often praised as an all-in-one solution for beginners seeking immediate cinematic results. It features two distinct mix options: "Modern," which offers a processed, punchy sound suitable for film scores, and "Traditional," which provides a drier, more natural acoustic character [1].

"Round Robins" are a technical sampling function that enhances realism by cycling through multiple recordings of the same note at the same velocity. When the user repeats a note, the sampler plays a slightly different variation each time, preventing the "machine gun" effect where identical samples create an artificial, repetitive artifact [2].

Composers typically use three workflows to switch between articulations in a DAW. First, **Key Switching** involves playing specific keys on the keyboard, usually below the playing range, to trigger a new articulation patch. Second, **Velocity Switching** maps different articulations to velocity ranges, so playing harder or softer changes the sound. Third, **MIDI CC Automation** uses Continuous Controller messages (like CC1 or CC20) to select or blend articulations via automation lanes or physical knobs on a controller [3].

## References
[1] Native Instruments. "Action Strings 2 - Manual." *Native Instruments Documentation*, native-instruments.com.
[2] EastWest Sounds. "What are Round Robins?" *EastWest ComposerCloud*, eastwestsounds.com.
[3] Spitfire Audio. "Key Switches and Articulations." *Spitfire Audio Help Centre*, spitfireaudio.com.
rounds.answer_b:
**Nucleus by Audio Imperia** is the specific all-in-one orchestral sample library recommended for beginners, featuring two distinct mix options: a modern (processed) sound and a traditional one, ideal for experimenting with hybrid and classical styles.[1]

Round Robins (RRs) enhance sample realism by recording multiple takes of the same note or articulation—typically 2-8 variations—and cycling through them automatically when a note is repeated. This prevents the "machine-gun effect," where identical samples create unnatural repetition, delivering more lifelike, organic performances akin to real musicians.[1]

Composers switch between instrument articulations (e.g., legato, staccato, pizzicato) in a DAW using three main workflows:  
- **Keyswitches:** Assign MIDI notes (e.g., C0-C2) below the playable range to trigger specific articulations; pressing a keyswitch loads the desired patch instantly.[1]  
- **Velocity switching:** Program higher/lower MIDI velocities (e.g., 1-64 for shorts, 65-127 for longs) to select articulations based on note strength.[1]  
- **CC lanes/mod wheel:** Map a continuous controller (e.g., CC1 mod wheel or CC11 expression) in the DAW's automation lane to morph between articulations smoothly during playback.[1]

These features make Nucleus resource-friendly for laptops, covering strings, brass, woodwinds, percussion, choir, solos, and cinematic design, with full section control.[1][2]

(Word count: 218)

## References
[1] https://www.becomeaprocomposer.com/guide/sample-libraries  
[2] https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RIQEacXEKFo
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent B correctly identified the specific library (Nucleus by Audio Imperia) that fits the 'all-in-one' and 'Modern vs Traditional mix' description. Agent A identified Action Strings 2, which is a phrase-based string library, not an all-in-one orchestral library (lacking brass, woodwinds, percussion, etc.), constituting a Deep Logic failure. Both agents interpreted the 'workflows' prompt by listing technical trigger methods (Keyswitches, Velocity, CC) rather than structural workflows (Separate Tracks, Expression Maps), but Agent B's accuracy on the core entity makes it the clear winner.
